sections.content: You will build a cricket match commentary generation system that leverages transformer-based tokenization, embedding layers, and attention mechanisms to produce contextually accurate match descriptions. This project demonstrates how large language models process sequential data, maintain context across variable-length inputs, and generate coherent outputs through token prediction. You'll implement a lightweight version that tokenizes cricket terminology (bowler names, deliveries, runs, wickets), encodes these tokens into dense vector representations, and uses attention-weighted mechanisms to predict the next commentary phrase based on historical sequences. This exercise reinforces core LLM concepts: the encoder-decoder architecture, the role of embedding dimension in capturing semantic relationships, and how attention enables models to selectively focus on relevant context when generating responses.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: A cricket match commentary requires understanding the entire match history—every delivery, every run, every wicket—to predict what happens next. Just as a seasoned commentator like Harsha Bhogle watches the field state (batter positioning, bowler's angle, field placement, match situation), recalls previous similar situations (how Virat Kohli performs against short-pitched bowling in the powerplay), and predicts the next delivery type, an LLM watches the token sequence fed to it, recalls patterns from training (how tokens cluster together in meaning), and predicts which token should come next. The commentator's expertise comes from having observed thousands of innings; the LLM's capability comes from having processed billions of tokens. When Bhogle says 'given the field setting and bowler's recent patterns, I predict a yorker,' he's doing exactly what the attention mechanism does—weighting which past observations matter most for the current prediction. Understanding this parallel reveals why LLMs need such vast training data and computational power: they're essentially learning to 'commentate' across any domain, and like Bhogle, they improve their predictions by remembering and weighing the most relevant context.

items: Proficiency in Python 3.9+ with NumPy/pandas for numerical operations and data manipulation
Understanding of neural network layers (Dense, Embedding) and forward pass mechanics in PyTorch or TensorFlow
Basic knowledge of attention mechanisms and softmax normalization for probability distributions
Familiarity with text tokenization concepts and vocabulary building from raw text corpora
Working installation of PyTorch 2.0+ or TensorFlow 2.12+ with CUDA support (optional but recommended)

sections.content: Initialize a structured Python project with dedicated directories for data, models, and utilities. Create a virtual environment to isolate dependencies, then install PyTorch, NumPy, and utility libraries. The project follows a modular architecture: a tokenizer module converts raw cricket commentary into integer tokens, an embedding module maps tokens to dense vectors, an attention module implements the core mechanism, and a generator module orchestrates prediction. This separation ensures each layer of the LLM pipeline can be tested independently, mirroring production ML practices. Configuration files store hyperparameters (embedding dimension, attention heads, vocabulary size), making experiments reproducible.

#!/bin/bash
# Create cricket match commentary LLM project structure

mkdir -p cricket_llm_project
cd cricket_llm_project

# Create virtual environment
python3 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate  # On Windows: venv\Scripts\activate

# Install dependencies
pip install torch torchvision torchaudio --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu118
pip install numpy pandas matplotlib scikit-learn jupyter

# Create project directory structure
mkdir -p {data,models,src/{tokenizer,embedding,attention,generator},tests,notebooks}

# Create essential files
touch src/__init__.py
touch src/tokenizer/__init__.py
touch src/embedding/__init__.py
touch src/attention/__init__.py
touch src/generator/__init__.py
touch config.yaml
touch requirements.txt
touch README.md

# Create data directory with sample cricket commentary
mkdir -p data/raw data/processed

echo "Project structure initialized successfully!"
echo "Directory tree:"
ls -R

Step 1 establishes the tokenization layer—the critical first component that converts raw cricket commentary strings into sequences of integer tokens. Tokenization is the bridge between human-readable text and mathematical tensors that neural networks process. You will build a vocabulary from sample cricket commentary (player names, actions, descriptive phrases), assign unique integer IDs to each token, and implement bidirectional encoding (text-to-tokens) and decoding (tokens-to-text). This foundation is essential because the LLM's learning capacity directly depends on vocabulary design: too small and you lose nuance; too large and you waste parameters on rare tokens. The tokenizer must handle cricket-specific terminology (yorker, bouncer, maidens, DRS, powerplay) properly, treating these as atomic units rather than character-level decompositions. Proper tokenization dramatically improves downstream model efficiency and semantic coherence.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Tokenization is like the cricket scorer noting down each delivery on the scorecard. A raw match unfolding in real-time contains infinite detail—the exact angle of the bowler's arm, the precise friction between bat and ball—but the scorer reduces this chaos into discrete symbols: 'W' for wicket, '4' for boundary, '.' for dot ball, 'nb' for no-ball. Just as the scorer's vocabulary (the set of symbols recognized on the scorecard) must include every event type that can occur, the tokenizer's vocabulary must include every meaningful unit of the LLM's domain. When a commentator later reads the scorecard to reconstruct the match, they convert these scored symbols back into flowing narrative. Similarly, the LLM decoder will convert token sequences back into readable commentary. The genius of proper tokenization—like a well-designed scorecard—is choosing the right granularity: you don't want 'Jasprit Bumrah' split into 'Jas', 'prit', 'Bum', 'rah' (too granular), nor as a single monolithic token never seen before (too coarse). Choose the right symbol set and reconstruction becomes seamless; choose poorly and even perfect downstream logic will produce garbled output.

# src/tokenizer/cricket_tokenizer.py
import re
from typing import List, Dict, Tuple
from collections import Counter

class CricketTokenizer:
    """
    Tokenizes cricket match commentary into integer sequences.
    Handles cricket-specific terminology and maintains bidirectional encoding.
    """
    
    def __init__(self
sections.min_frequency:
int = 2):
        """
        Initialize tokenizer with vocabulary constraints.
        
        Args:
            vocab_size: Maximum vocabulary size
            min_frequency: Minimum occurrences for token inclusion
        """
        self.vocab_size = vocab_size
        self.min_frequency = min_frequency
        self.vocab = {}  # token_string -> token_id
        self.reverse_vocab = {}  # token_id -> token_string
        self.token_counts = Counter()
        self.next_token_id = 0
        
        # Special tokens
        self.PAD_TOKEN = "<PAD>"
        self.UNK_TOKEN = "<UNK>"
        self.START_TOKEN = "<START>"
        self.END_TOKEN = "<END>"
        
        self._initialize_special_tokens()
    
    def _initialize_special_tokens(self):
        """Reserve token IDs for special tokens."""
        for token in [self.PAD_TOKEN, self.UNK_TOKEN, self.START_TOKEN, self.END_TOKEN]:
            self.vocab[token] = self.next_token_id
            self.reverse_vocab[self.next_token_id] = token
            self.next_token_id += 1
    
    def _preprocess_text(self
sections.text:
str) -> List[str]:
        """
        Preprocess cricket commentary: lowercase, split into meaningful units.
        Cricket terms like 'yorker', 'bouncer', 'DRS' are kept intact.
        """
        # Convert to lowercase
        text = text.lower()
        
        # Replace punctuation except apostrophe
        text = re.sub(r"[^a-z0-9'\s]", " ", text)
        
        # Split on whitespace
        tokens = text.split()
        
        return tokens
    
    def build_vocab(self
sections.texts:
List[str]):
        """
        Build vocabulary from corpus of cricket commentary.
        
        Args:
            texts: List of raw commentary strings
        """
        # Count token frequencies
        for text in texts:
            tokens = self._preprocess_text(text)
            self.token_counts.update(tokens)
        
        # Add tokens meeting frequency threshold
        for token, count in self.token_counts.most_common(self.vocab_size):
            if count >= self.min_frequency and token not in self.vocab:
                self.vocab[token] = self.next_token_id
                self.reverse_vocab[self.next_token_id] = token
                self.next_token_id += 1
    
    def encode(self

sections.text:
str) -> List[int]:
        """
        Convert text to token IDs.
        
        Args:
            text: Raw commentary string
            
        Returns:
            List of integer token IDs
        """
        tokens = self._preprocess_text(text)
        token_ids = []
        
        for token in tokens:
            if token in self.vocab:
                token_ids.append(self.vocab[token])
            else:
                # Out-of-vocabulary words map to UNK token
                token_ids.append(self.vocab[self.UNK_TOKEN])
        
        return token_ids
    
    def decode(self
sections.token:
str) -> int:
        """Get ID for a specific token, or UNK if not in vocab."""
        return self.vocab.get(token, self.vocab[self.UNK_TOKEN])


# Example usage and testing
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Sample cricket commentary
    cricket_commentary = [
        "Rohit Sharma takes a single to mid wicket. Virat Kohli on strike.",
        "Jasprit Bumrah bowls a yorker. Sharma plays back foot defense.",
        "Short pitched delivery outside off stump. Caught at slip by Bumrah.",
        "Powerplay wicket! Root departs caught in the deep.",
        "Maiden over from Shami. Excellent bowling spell.",
        "DRS review requested. Hawk eye shows three reds. Bowled!",
        "Bumrah to Bairstow. Bouncer on leg stump. Handled confidently."
    ]
    
    # Initialize and build tokenizer
    tokenizer = CricketTokenizer(vocab_size=5000, min_frequency=1)
    tokenizer.build_vocab(cricket_commentary)
    
    print(f"Vocabulary size: {tokenizer.get_vocab_size()}")
    print(f"Sample vocabulary: {list(tokenizer.vocab.items())[:10]}")
    
    # Test encoding and decoding
    test_text = "Rohit Sharma hits a boundary off Bumrah"
    encoded = tokenizer.encode(test_text)
    decoded = tokenizer.decode(encoded)
    
    print(f"\nOriginal: {test_text}")
    print(f"Encoded: {encoded}")
    print(f"Decoded: {decoded}")

sections.content: Step 2 implements the embedding and attention mechanisms—the computational heart of the LLM. Embeddings transform discrete token IDs into dense, continuous vectors where semantically similar tokens (e.g., 'bowler' and 'pitcher') occupy nearby regions in vector space. The embedding dimension (typically 512-2048 in production models) controls model expressiveness and capacity. Attention mechanisms learn which tokens in the input sequence are most relevant for predicting the next token, using scaled dot-product attention: compute similarity scores between the query (current token) and all keys (previous tokens), apply softmax to create probability weights, then aggregate values (token representations) using these weights. This architecture allows the model to form long-range dependencies—understanding that 'Bumrah' at position 5 strongly influences the prediction at position 20 because they're semantically linked in context. Scaled-dot-product attention ensures numerical stability and makes gradient flow efficient during backpropagation.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: The embedding mechanism is like storing each player's playing profile—not as a bare name, but as a rich vector of attributes: scoring rate against pace, weaknesses to spin, fielding ability, form in last 5 innings, age, height, handedness. Virat Kohli's vector is far from Rohit Sharma's because their playing styles differ fundamentally, but both are 'batter' vectors with overlap in that region of space. Now, during a live match, the commentary generator needs to predict: 'Bumrah bowls a... [what type of delivery]?' The attention mechanism is the captain's decision-making. The captain attends to (pays close attention to) the most relevant context: How has this batter played against short-pitched bowling before (look back at previous deliveries)? What's the match situation—is it powerplay or death overs? What's Bumrah's recent bowling performance this spell? The captain weighs each piece of context (via softmax probability scores: 80% weight on recent form, 15% on historical matchup, 5% on general strategy). Similarly, attention computes: which past tokens in the commentary sequence are most relevant for predicting the next token? Then it blends those relevant tokens together, weighted by relevance. This reveals why attention is transformative: it's not a fixed hand-coded rule but a learned weighting that adapts based on what the data reveals matters most for each prediction.

# src/embedding/embedding_layer.py
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import math

class CricketEmbedding(nn.Module):
    """
    Token embedding layer that maps discrete token IDs to continuous vectors.
    Captures semantic relationships between cricket terminology.
    """
    
    def __init__(self
sections.pad_idx:
int = 0):
        """
        Initialize embedding layer.
        
        Args:
            vocab_size: Size of vocabulary
            embedding_dim: Dimension of embedding vectors
            pad_idx: Index of padding token (gradients won't be computed)
        """
        super().__init__()
        self.embedding = nn.Embedding(
            num_embeddings=vocab_size,
            embedding_dim=embedding_dim,
            padding_idx=pad_idx
        )
        # Scale embeddings to prevent vanishing gradients
        self.scale = math.sqrt(embedding_dim)
    
    def forward(self
sections.token_ids:
torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """
        Convert token IDs to embedding vectors.
        
        Args:
            token_ids: Tensor of shape (batch_size, seq_length)
            
        Returns:
            Embedding tensor of shape (batch_size, seq_length, embedding_dim)
        """
        embeddings = self.embedding(token_ids)
        # Scale embeddings for numerical stability
        return embeddings * self.scale


class ScaledDotProductAttention(nn.Module):
    """
    Scaled dot-product attention mechanism.
    Learns which parts of the input sequence are most relevant for each position.
    """
    
    def __init__(self

sections.dropout:
float = 0.1):
        """
        Initialize attention mechanism.
        
        Args:
            embedding_dim: Dimension of key/query/value vectors
            dropout: Dropout rate for attention weights
        """
        super().__init__()
        self.embedding_dim = embedding_dim
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)
        self.scale = math.sqrt(embedding_dim)
    
    def forward(self
sections.mask:
torch.Tensor = None) -> tuple:
        """
        Compute scaled dot-product attention.
        
        Args:
            query: Tensor of shape (batch_size, ..., seq_length_q, embedding_dim)
            key: Tensor of shape (batch_size, ..., seq_length_k, embedding_dim)
            value: Tensor of shape (batch_size, ..., seq_length_v, embedding_dim)
            mask: Optional mask to prevent attention to certain positions
            
        Returns:
            Tuple of (output, attention_weights)
        """
        # Compute attention scores: Q * K^T
        scores = torch.matmul(query, key.transpose(-2, -1)) / self.scale
        
        # Apply mask if provided (prevent attending to future positions)
        if mask is not None:
            scores = scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, float('-inf'))
        
        # Convert scores to attention weights via softmax
        attention_weights = torch.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
        attention_weights = self.dropout(attention_weights)
        
        # Aggregate values using attention weights
        output = torch.matmul(attention_weights, value)
        
        return output, attention_weights


class CricketAttentionHead(nn.Module):
    """
    Single attention head that learns one type of context relationship.
    Multiple heads capture different semantic aspects of cricket terminology.
    """
    
    def __init__(self

sections.dropout:
float = 0.1):
        """
        Initialize attention head.
        
        Args:
            embedding_dim: Full embedding dimension
            head_dim: Dimension for this head (embedding_dim / num_heads)
            dropout: Dropout rate
        """
        super().__init__()
        self.head_dim = head_dim
        
        # Linear projections for query, key, value
        self.query_proj = nn.Linear(embedding_dim, head_dim)
        self.key_proj = nn.Linear(embedding_dim, head_dim)
        self.value_proj = nn.Linear(embedding_dim, head_dim)
        
        # Attention mechanism
        self.attention = ScaledDotProductAttention(head_dim, dropout)
        
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)
    
    def forward(self
sections.mask:
torch.Tensor = None) -> tuple:
        """
        Apply attention head.
        
        Args:
            query, key
sections.value:
Input tensors
            mask: Attention mask
            
        Returns:
            Tuple of (head_output, attention_weights)
        """
        # Project inputs
        Q = self.query_proj(query)
        K = self.key_proj(key)
        V = self.value_proj(value)
        
        # Apply attention
        output, weights = self.attention(Q, K, V, mask)
        
        return output, weights


class MultiHeadAttention(nn.Module):
    """
    Multi-head attention: multiple attention heads attending in parallel.
    Each head learns different contextual relationships in cricket commentary.
    Example: one head might focus on player relationships, another on delivery types.
    """
    
    def __init__(self

sections.dropout:
float = 0.1):
        """
        Initialize multi-head attention.
        
        Args:
            embedding_dim: Full embedding dimension
            num_heads: Number of parallel attention heads
            dropout: Dropout rate
        """
        super().__init__()
        assert embedding_dim % num_heads == 0, "embedding_dim must be divisible by num_heads"
        
        self.embedding_dim = embedding_dim
        self.num_heads = num_heads
        self.head_dim = embedding_dim // num_heads
        
        # Create multiple attention heads
        self.heads = nn.ModuleList([
            CricketAttentionHead(embedding_dim, self.head_dim, dropout)
            for _ in range(num_heads)
        ])
        
        # Output projection to combine all heads
        self.output_proj = nn.Linear(embedding_dim, embedding_dim)
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)
    
    def forward(self
sections.mask:
torch.Tensor = None) -> tuple:
        """
        Apply multi-head attention.
        
        Args:
            query, key
sections.value:
Input tensors
            mask: Attention mask
            
        Returns:
            Tuple of (output, attention_weights)
        """
        # Collect outputs from all heads
        head_outputs = []
        all_attention_weights = []
        
        for head in self.heads:
            output, weights = head(query, key, value, mask)
            head_outputs.append(output)
            all_attention_weights.append(weights)
        
        # Concatenate all head outputs
        combined = torch.cat(head_outputs, dim=-1)
        
        # Project combined output
        output = self.output_proj(combined)
        output = self.dropout(output)
        
        # Average attention weights across heads for visualization
        avg_weights = torch.stack(all_attention_weights, dim=0).mean(dim=0)
        
        return output, avg_weights


# Example usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Configuration
    vocab_size = 1000
    embedding_dim = 256
    num_heads = 8
    seq_length = 10
    batch_size = 2
    
    # Initialize modules
    embedding_layer = CricketEmbedding(vocab_size, embedding_dim)
    attention_layer = MultiHeadAttention(embedding_dim, num_heads)
    
    # Create sample token IDs (cricket player names, bowling deliveries)
    token_ids = torch.randint(0, vocab_size, (batch_size, seq_length))
    
    # Forward pass
    embeddings = embedding_layer(token_ids)
    print(f"Embedding shape: {embeddings.shape}")
    
    # Self-attention: query=key=value=embeddings
    output, attention_weights = attention_layer(embeddings, embeddings, embeddings)
    print(f"Attention output shape: {output.shape}")
    print(f"Attention weights shape: {attention_weights.shape}")
    print(f"\nSample attention weights (first batch, first position):")
    print(attention_weights[0, 0, :].detach().numpy())

sections.content: Step 3 integrates the tokenizer, embedding, and attention layers into a complete commentary generator that accepts match context (player names, bowling type, match phase) and produces coherent multi-token commentary sequences. The generator implements teacher forcing during training (where true previous tokens guide the model) and autoregressive generation during inference (where model predictions feed back as input). You'll add positional encoding to preserve sequence order information—critical because attention alone is permutation-invariant but cricket commentary depends heavily on temporal sequence. The complete model includes a feed-forward network after attention, layer normalization for training stability, and residual connections for gradient flow. Testing uses cricket-specific metrics: commentary coherence (do generated sequences make tactical sense?), semantic appropriateness (does predicted delivery type match field setup?), and diversity (does the model avoid repetitive patterns?). This integration transforms individual components into a functional end-to-end system.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Assembling the complete LLM is like orchestrating a full cricket team. The tokenizer is your opening partnership—it establishes the foundation, converts chaos (raw match flow) into meaningful units (scorecard entries). The embeddings are your middle-order batters—they add depth and context, representing each element richly. The attention mechanism is your bowling attack—it strategically focuses on relevant weaknesses and opportunities, adapting to match conditions. Positional encoding is the field captain's tactical awareness—knowing that the 10th over of the powerplay is different from the 40th over, or that a wicket-down situation changes everything. The feed-forward networks are your fielders—applying judgment and strategy based on what they observe. Layer normalization is your physio—ensuring steady, stable performance without erratic swings. Residual connections are your team's bench strength—allowing alternative paths of communication if the primary strategy gets blocked. When you integrate all these, you create a team that can watch a live match situation (input tokens), reason about what's tactically sound next (attention + feed-forward), and articulate that reasoning in natural language (decode back to commentary). Just as a strong team beats an assembly of talented individuals, integrating all components creates emergent capability—the system can handle novel match situations never seen in training.
